coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent biosensor based on magnetic separation platform and spore-like breakable organosilica nanocapsules controlled-release carbon dots for the detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ling, Zhiming
Xu, Qian
Song, Yang
Zhang, Wanqing
Xu, Hengyi
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasensitive and rapid detection of low concentration of Escherichia coli O157: H7 (E. coli O157:H7) in food is essential for food safety and public health. In this study, A novel fluorescence signal amplification biosensor based on magnetic separation platform and red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs)-encapsulated breakable organosilica nanocapsules (BONs) for ultrasensitive detection of E. coli O157:H7 was established. Wulff-type boronic acid functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs@B–N/APBA) with broad-spectrum bacterial recognition ability were synthesized for the first time to recognize and capture E. coli O157: H7 in food samples. R-CDs@BONs labeled with anti-E. coli O157:H7 monoclonal antibody (mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2) were used as the second recognition element to ensure the specificity for E. coli O157:H7 and form MNPs@B–N/APBA∼ E. coli O157:H7∼mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 sandwich complexes, followed by releasing R-CDs to generate amplified fluorescence response signals for quantitative detection of E. coli O157:H7. The proposed method had a limit of detection with 25 CFU/mL in pure culture and contaminated lettuce samples, which the whole detection process took about 120 min. This fluorescence signal amplification biosensor has the potential to detect other pathogens in food by altering specific antibodies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Wulff-type boronic acid
Magnetic nanoparticles
Carbon dots-encapsulated nanocapsules
Fluorescence signal amplification biosensors
E. coli O157:H7

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure of MNPs@B–N/APBA to separate E. coli O157:H7
Detection of E. coli O157:H7 in spiked food samples
Principles of fluorescence signal amplification method for detecting E. coli O157:H7
Synthesized MNPs@B–N/APBA and mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 bind to E. coli O157:H7
Optimization of detection conditions for the proposed method
Capability and stability of MNPs@B–N/APBA to capture E. coli O157:H7
Specificity of fluorescence signal amplification method for detecting E. coli O157:H7
Evaluation the performance of fluorescence signal amplification assay for detecting E. coli O157:H7 in spiked food samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful to the Research Project of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University (Project No. SKLF-ZZB-202328) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of R-CDs. (A) TEM of R-CDs (Inset: the particle size distribution of R-CDs). (B) High-resolution TEM of R-CDs. (C) XPS wide-scan spectra of R-CDs. (D–G) High-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and S 2p. (H) Fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of R-CDs (Inset: images of R-CDs solution under natural and UV light). (I) Fluorescence decay curve (dot plot) and fitting curve (solid line) of R-CDs.

The characterization of R-CDs@BONs-NH2. (A) TEM of R-CDs@BONs-NH2 (Inset: the particle size distribution of R-CDs@BONs-NH2). (B) FT-IR spectra of R-CDs and R-CDs@BONs-NH2. (C) XPS wide-scan spectra of R-CDs@BONs-NH2. (D–F) High-resolution XPS spectra of Si 2p, N 1s and O 1s.

Capability and stability of MNPs@B–N/APBA to capture E. coli O157:H7. (A) CE of different bacteria by MNPs@B–N/APBA. (B) CE of E. coli O157:H7 by MNPs@B–N/APBA in different bacterial mixtures, Mixture 1 including E. coli O157:H7 (105 CFU/mL) and S. enteritidis (105 CFU/mL), Mixture 2 including E. coli O157:H7 (104 CFU/mL) and S. enteritidis (105 CFU/mL), Mixture 3 including E. coli O157:H7 (105 CFU/mL) and S. aureus (105 CFU/mL), Mixture 4 including E. coli O157:H7 (104 CFU/mL) and S. aureus (105 CFU/mL), Mixture 5 including E. coli O157:H7 (105 CFU/mL), S. aureus (105 CFU/mL) and S. enteritidis (105 CFU/mL), Mixture 6 including E. coli O157:H7 (104 CFU/mL), S. aureus (105 CFU/mL) and S. enteritidis (105 CFU/mL). (C) CE of E. coli O157:H7 (2.5 × 101 to 2.5 × 106 CFU/mL) in spiked samples by MNPs@B–N/APBA. Effects of pH (D), NaCl concentration (E) and storage time of MNPs@B–N/APBA (F) on the CE of E. coli O157:H7 by MNPs@B–N/APBA.

Specificity of the established method. The fluorescence spectra (A) and fluorescence quantitative results at 650 nm (B) of the established method. 1: Control, 2: S. aureus CMCC26001, 3: MRSA NCTC12493, 4: B. cereus JX-CDC JDZ0102Y, 5: L. monocytogenes ATCC13932, 6: S. typhimurium ATCC13311, 7: S. flexneri CMCC51572, 8: E. coli O157:H7 ATCC43888, 9: mixture of E. coli O157:H7 ATCC43888, S. aureus CMCC26001 and S. enteritidis ATCC13076. The concentrations of bacteria were 105 CFU/mL.

Sensitivity of the established method. Fluorescence spectra (A) and standard curve (B) for different concentrations (101–106 CFU/mL) of E. coli O157:H7 in PBS by the established method. Fluorescence spectra (C) and standard curve (D) for different concentrations (101–106 CFU/mL) of E. coli O157:H7 in spiked lettuce samples by the established method.

Schematic illustration of the fluorescence signal amplification assay for detecting E. coli O157:H7. (A) Preparation of Wulff-type boronic acid and MNPs@B–N/APBA. (B) Preparation of mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2. (C) Detection principles of the fluorescence signal amplification assay.
Comparison of current biosensor-based methods for detection of E. coli O157:H7 in food.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent biosensor based on magnetic separation platform and spore-like breakable organosilica nanocapsules controlled-release carbon dots for the detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330047, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330047, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Institute of Food Innovation Co., Ltd., Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330200, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Institute of Food Innovation Co., Ltd.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Institute of Food Innovation Co., Ltd., Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330200, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University. Address: 235 Nanjing East Road, Nanchang 330047, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasensitive and rapid detection of low concentration of Escherichia coli O157: H7 (E. coli O157:H7) in food is essential for food safety and public health. In this study, A novel fluorescence signal amplification biosensor based on magnetic separation platform and red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs)-encapsulated breakable organosilica nanocapsules (BONs) for ultrasensitive detection of E. coli O157:H7 was established. Wulff-type boronic acid functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs@B–N/APBA) with broad-spectrum bacterial recognition ability were synthesized for the first time to recognize and capture E. coli O157: H7 in food samples. R-CDs@BONs labeled with anti-E. coli O157:H7 monoclonal antibody (mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2) were used as the second recognition element to ensure the specificity for E. coli O157:H7 and form MNPs@B–N/APBA∼ E. coli O157:H7∼mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 sandwich complexes, followed by releasing R-CDs to generate amplified fluorescence response signals for quantitative detection of E. coli O157:H7. The proposed method had a limit of detection with 25 CFU/mL in pure culture and contaminated lettuce samples, which the whole detection process took about 120 min. This fluorescence signal amplification biosensor has the potential to detect other pathogens in food by altering specific antibodies.

In this study, we establish a novel fluorescence signal amplification biosensor for ultrasensitive detection of E. coli O157:H7 in food.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7) is one of the most common serotypes of enterohaemorrhagic E. coli strains with strong environmental adaptability, which is widely found in vegetables, fruit juices, dairy products and other contaminated foods [1]. E. coli O157:H7 infection has strong pathogenicity and can cause grievous illnesses such as thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, hemorrhagic colitis, and life-threatening hemolytic uremic syndrome through a small number of bacteria (10–100 CFU) [2]. Therefore, establishing a sensitive, rapid and specific method for detecting E. coli O157:H7 is essential for rapid screening of infection and ensuring food safety.

To date, the most widely available and highly specific methods for the detection of foodborne pathogens include traditional culture method, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [3] and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [4], but they require a time-consuming identification process or a rather cumbersome procedure to achieve high detection sensitivity [5]. Compared with traditional detection methods, emerging biosensors [6] based on biorecognition molecules (such as antibodies, aptamers and boronic acid) [7,8] can quickly convert the concentration of pathogenic bacteria into observable physical or chemical signals, such as fluorescence, colorimetric or electrical signals, resulting in rapid detection results. Among them, the fluorescent biosensor is used to detect pathogenic bacteria with the advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity and fast response time [5]. Moreover, carbon dots (CDs) are widely used to construct fluorescent biosensors with excellent performance due to their good modifiability, excellent fluorescence properties, good biocompatibility and photobleaching resistance [9]. However, since complex matrices or small molecules in food can affect the fluorescence properties of CDs [10], CDs-based fluorescent biosensors usually need to be combined with some bacterial separation methods to improve the specificity and sensitivity of the detection [11]. Bacterial enrichment and separation can be accomplished by conventional centrifugation and filtration operations, but due to the simultaneous presence of large particles and small molecules in the food matrix, these methods are time-consuming and less effective for enrichment. Development of nanotechnology has transformed the pre-processing procedures for bioanalysis. Of which, the magnetic separation technology based on modified magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) has been widely explored and applied in bacterial separation, with the advantages of high bacterial enrichment efficiency and fast speed. Therefore, the combination of magnetic separation technology with CDs-based biosensors can effectively eliminate the interference of food matrices and has great potential for application in bacterial detection [12].

However, the use of magnetic separation combined with CD-based fluorescent biosensors for immunoassays of foodborne pathogens has some drawbacks. For example, the size of MNPs and bacteria is much larger than that of CDs, which will lead to the partial blocking of the excitation and emission light of CDs, resulting in a decrease in detection sensitivity [13]. Futhermore, since MNPs have a relatively wide range of UV–Vis absorption, the fluorescent signal of CDs may be partially absorbed [14]. Therefore, the investigation of new analytical methods to achieve the release of CDs before fluorescence detection can cleverly solve the above shortcomings and improve the sensitivity of the detection. In recent years, nanomaterials with high loading capacity, such as nanocapsules [15], nanoflowers [16], and metal-organic frameworks [17], have been intensively investigated and applied to drug delivery, bioimaging, and biosensing. In particular, silica nanocapsules have attracted great attention due to their mild synthesis conditions and strong protective effect on encapsulated nanoparticles [18]. Therefore, silica nanocapsules were used to encapsulate and release CDs, which could improve the stability of foodborne pathogen detection and amplify the fluorescence signal.

Herein, based on the recognition effect of boronic acid on bacterial lipopolysaccharide (LPS) [8], Wulff-type 3-aminophenylboric acid (B–N/APBA) functionalized MNPs was synthesized as capture probes (MNPs@B–N/APBA) to enrich and separate E. coli O157:H7 in food samples. By using red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs)-encapsulated breakable organosilica nanocapsules (BONs) as specific spore-like detection probes, the labeling and release of R-CDs on the surface of E. coli O157:H7 captured by MNPs@B–N/APBA were achieved, resulting in amplified fluorescent signals for the sensitive detection of E. coli O157:H7. The advantages of this strategy are as follows: Firstly, B–N/APBA-modified MNPs had a high affinity for E. coli O157:H7, with the capture efficiency (CE) above 84% for low-abundance E. coli O157:H7 (103 CFU/mL) in spiked lettuce samples. Secondly, the synthesized BONs can effectively encapsulate and release R-CDs to amplify the detected fluorescence signal. Thirdly, the combination of magnetic separation technology and R-CDs@BONs can eliminate the influence of food matrix and MNPs on the fluorescence performance of R-CDs, thereby improving the sensitivity of E. coli O157:H7 detection.
The materials and reagents, preparation of capture probes and detection probes in the experiment are provided in the Supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure of MNPs@B–N/APBA to separate E. coli O157:H7
ce:section.ce:para:
The procedure of MNPs@B–N/APBA to separate E. coli O157:H7 was illustrated in Scheme 1 C. 90 μg of MNPs@B–N/APBA were added into 1 mL PBS with E. coli O157:H7 range from 2.5 × 101–2.5 × 106 CFU/mL. After incubation for 40 min in a shaker at 37°C, the MNPs@B–N/APBA∼ E. coli O157:H7 complexes were collected under magnetic field for 5 min and resuspended in 1 mL PBS. And uncaptured E. coli O157:H7, which presented in solution after magnetic separation, were transferred into a new centrifuge tube. Subsequently, the collected MNPs@B–N/APBA∼ E. coli O157:H7 complexes and uncaptured E. coli O157:H7 were spread on Modified Sorbitol MacConkey agar plates for culturing and counting. The following equation was used to calculate capture efficiency (CE):
(1)
CE
( % )
=
[
C
/
(
C + N
)
]
× 100 %
C represents the colony number of captured bacteria and N represents the colony number of uncaptured bacteria.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of E. coli O157:H7 in spiked food samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of the proposed strategy for detecting E. coli O157:H7 in real food samples, detection experiments were performed in spiked fruit juices and lettuce by fluorescence method. Specifically, spiked food samples containing different concentrations of bacteria were obtained by adding E. coli O157:H7 (2.5 × 100–2.5 × 106 CFU/mL) to sterilized food samples. Then, added 90 μg of MNPs@B–N/APBA into 1 mL spiked sample and incubated for 40 min at 37°C. The MNPs@B–N/APBA∼ E. coli O157:H7 complexes were collected from the sample under magnetic field and resuspended in 1 mL PBS containing 1% (w/v) BSA solution to block nonspecific absorption. After removing excess BSA through magnetic separation, the MNPs@B–N/APBA∼ E. coli O157:H7 complexes were resuspended in 1 mL sterile PBS, then added 40 μg of mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 and incubated for 15 min at 37°C. After incubation, washed twice times with 1 mL PBS by magnetic separation to remove unbound mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 and obtained MNPs@B–N/APBA∼ E. coli O157:H7∼mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 sandwich complexes and resuspended them in 0.2 mL PBS. Finally, 0.75 mg NaBH4 was added and incubated in a shaker for 20 min. After incubation, the released R-CDs in supernatant were collected by magnetic separation, and the fluorescence value of 180 μL of R-CDs supernatant was measured by a microplate reader at excitation wavelength (Ex) of 425 nm and emission wavelength (Em) of 650 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principles of fluorescence signal amplification method for detecting E. coli O157:H7
ce:section.ce:para: The procedures for the enrichment and fluorescence signal amplification assays of E. coli O157:H7 were illustrated in Scheme 1. Since APBA can be conjugated to LPS with cis-diol groups on the bacterial surface by a reversible esterification reaction [19], it can be used as a recognition molecule for bacteria. However, food samples tend to occur in acidic or neutral conditions, making the affinity recognition of APBA unsatisfactory in real samples. Based on this, we improved APBA to form new boronic acid ligands (Wulff-type boronic acid), reducing their binding pH to bacteria [20]. The synthesis process of MNPs@B–N/APBA is shown in Scheme 1A, B–N/APBA was successfully synthesized through the intermolecular B–N coordination bond formed by the self-assembly of boronic acid and amine (teamed boronate affinity strategy) and modified on the surface of the MNPs by carbodiimide reaction as capture probes for E. coli O157:H7. To achieve fluorescence quantification of this method, R-CDs were prepared using GSH and formamide. Considering the effect of MNPs on R-CDs and the specificity of the method, we synthesized mAb-labeled R-CDs@BONs-NH2 as spore-like detection probes (mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2) (Scheme 1B), which enabled the release of R-CDs and amplification of fluorescence signals through the sandwich strategy and the fragmentability of BONs (Scheme 1C). Eventually, the fluorescence values at Ex of 425 nm and Em of 650 nm were measured, respectively. Moreover, the positive correlation between the concentration of the target bacteria and the fluorescence value was obtained, realizing the ultrasensitive detection of E. coli O157:H7.

Firstly, MNPs and MNPs@B–N/APBA were characterized by dynamic light scattering (DLS). As shown in Figs. S1A–B, the average particle size of MNPs was 193.40 nm, and the average particle size of MNPs@B–N/APBA increased to 217.63 nm after modification with B–N/APBA. The surface potentials of MNPs and MNPs@B–N/APBA were −32.5 and −20.6 mV (Fig. S1C). The increase of potential was caused by the carbodiimide reaction between the amino group of B–N/APBA and the carboxyl group on the surface of MNPs during the modification process. Subsequently, the surface functional groups of MNPs and MNPs@B–N/APBA were investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. S1D). The absorption peak at 1646 cm−1 was related to the bending vibration of N–H in B–N/APBA. The absorption peak of the stretching vibration of B–O in B–N/APBA was located at 1453 cm−1. The absorption peaks at 1075 cm−1 and 700 cm−1 were attributed to the bending vibration of C–H in the phenyl and alkyl chains, respectively [19]. The FT-IR results demonstrated the successful modification of MNPs by B–N/APBA.

Subsequently, a series of characterizations were performed on the synthesized R-CDs. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of R-CDs in Fig. 1 A–B showed that R-CDs were quasi-spherical with an average particle size of 3.6 nm, and the high-resolution TEM image showed clear lattice fringes with a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the crystal plane of graphite (100), which indicated R-CDs have a graphite-like structure. Then, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and FT-IR were used to explore the chemical composition and structure of R-CDs. The full spectrum of XPS (Fig. 1C) showed that the four peaks of 530.1 eV, 399.0 eV, 284.2 eV, and 162.2 eV belong to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and S 2p, respectively, indicating that R-CDs are composed of four elements: C, N, O, and S. The HR XPS spectra of O 1s (Fig. 1D) showed the existence of C–O/C–OH (531.68 eV) and CO (530.13 eV) bonds on the surface of R-CDs. HR XPS C 1s (Fig. 1E) showed the presence of CO (287.37 eV), C–O (286.62 eV), C–S/C–N (284.80 eV), and C–C/CC (283.52 eV) bonds. The characteristic peaks at 399.86, 398.61, and 397.37 eV in HR XPS N 1s were attributed to N–H, pyrrole N, and pyridine N, respectively (Fig. 1F). The HR spectra of S 2p (Fig. 1G) showed the presence of four S types, which were oxidized S (167.23 eV), 2p1/2 (163.51 eV) of thiophene S, 2p3/2 (162.22 eV) of thiophene S, and thiolate (160.74 eV) [21]. These results indicated that R-CDs are doped with N and S. Then, the attribution of each characteristic peak of XPS was further verified by FT-IR spectra of R-CDs (Fig. 2 B). The wide peak at 3224 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups. The absorption peaks at 1660 and 1386 cm−1 were the stretching vibrations of CO and C–N in the amide bond, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1586, 1174 and 1004 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of C–N, C–O and C–S bonds, respectively [22]. Finally, the fluorescence properties of the synthesized R-CDs were comprehensively explored. As shown in Fig. 1H, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of R-CDs were 425 nm and 650 nm, respectively, and the optimal emission wavelength showed excitation-independent properties (Fig. S2). The fluorescence decay curve in Fig. 1I showed that the fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield of R-CDs at 425 nm excitation are 3.64 ns and 6.76%, respectively. The above results indicated that R-CDs was successfully synthesized and had excellent fluorescence properties.

In addition, the synthesis and modification of mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 were also verified. The TEM image in Fig. 2A shows that the morphology of the synthesized R-CDs@BONs-NH2 is quasi-spherical, with an average particle size of 57.3 nm. After encapsulation of R-CDs, the disappearance of the infrared characteristic peak of R-CDs and the appearance of new characteristic peaks preliminarily proved the successful synthesis of R-CDs@BONs-NH2 (Fig. 2B). The absorption peaks at 3357 and 1564 cm−1 were attributed to the N–H stretching and bending vibrations of the amino groups on the surface of R-CDs@BONs-NH2, respectively. The absorption peaks of antisymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of Si–O–Si were shown at 1034 and 782 cm−1, respectively. The absorption peak at 690 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C–S [23]. Subsequently, XPS was used to verify the microstructure of R-CDs@BONs-NH2. As shown in the full spectrum of XPS (Fig. 2C), R-CDs@BONs-NH2 had characteristic peaks of O 1s (531.0 eV), N 1s (400.0 eV), C 1s (284.0 eV), and Si 2p (101.5 eV). HR XPS spectra of Si 2p (Fig. 2D) showed the presence of Si–O (104.59 eV), Si–N (101.85 eV), and Si–C (101.06 eV) bonds on the surface of R-CDs@BONs-NH2. HR XPS N 1s (Fig. 2E) showed characteristic peaks of N–H (399.94 eV) and Si–N (397.77 eV) [24], which also demonstrated the success of surface amination of R-CDs@BONs. The characteristic peaks at 531.79, 530.88, and 529.64 eV in HR XPS O 1s were attributed to the Si–O, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 2F) [25]. Then, the Zeta potentials of R-CDs, R-CDs@BONs-NH2 and mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 were measured (Fig. S3), and their surface potentials were −15.7, −16.5 and −18.8 mV, respectively. The increase of the potentials proved the successful encapsulation of the R-CDs and the successful labeling of the mAb. Finally, the fluorescence spectra and fluorescence values at Ex of 425 nm and Em of 650 nm of mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 before and after being broken by NaBH4 were measured. The fluorescence signal of the solution after mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 being broken was 2.9-fold enhanced compared to before fragmentation (Fig. S4), indicating that the encapsulation and release of R-CDs can quench and recover their fluorescence to amplify the detection signals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesized MNPs@B–N/APBA and mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 bind to E. coli O157:H7
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the MNPs@B–N/APBA and mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 were determined by scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Figs. S5A–C). The synthesized MNPs@B–N/APBA and mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 were quasi-spherical with relatively uniform particle sizes. And the SEM images of E. coli O157:H7 (Fig. S5D), MNPs@B–N/APBA∼ E. coli O157:H7 (Fig. S5E) and MNPs@B–N/APBA∼ E. coli O157:H7∼mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 sandwich complexes (Fig. S5F), indicated that the synthesized MNPs@B–N/APBA and mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 could bind firmly to E. coli O157:H7, which proved that the proposed fluorescence signal amplification assay had the ability to detect E. coli O157:H7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental conditions, including magnetic separation parameters and detection procedures, were optimized to construct a sensitive fluorescent biosensor. First, the magnetic separation conditions were optimized to obtain more target bacteria using E. coli O157:H7 with a concentration of 104 CFU/mL. The CE of E. coli O157:H7 reached a plateau when the dosage of MNPs@B–N/APBA was 90 μg (Fig. S6A), the incubation time of MNPs@B–N/APBA with E. coli O157:H7 was 40 min (Fig. S6B), and the magnetic separation time was 5 min (Fig. S6C). Therefore, the optimized magnetic separation procedure for E. coli O157:H7 were as follows: 90 μg of MNPs@B–N/APBA were added into 1 mL sample, followed by 40 min of co-incubation with the target bacteria, and ultimately magnetic separation was performed for 5 min before the collection of MNPs@B–N/APBA∼ E. coli O157:H7 complexes.

In addition, the detection procedure is also an important factor affecting the detection sensitivity. In the detection procedure, the labeling of the mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 and the release of R-CDs significantly affect the fluorescence signal intensity of the established assay, which requires optimization of these detection conditions. As shown in Figs. S7A–D, when the dosage of mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 was 40 μg, the labeling time of mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 was 15 min, the dosage of NaBH4 was 0.75 mg, and the mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 fragmentation time was 20 min, the fluorescence signal was strong and reached the plateau. Therefore, in the subsequent experiments, the dosage of mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2, the labeling time of mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2, the dosage of NaBH4, and the fragmentation time of mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 were 40 μg, 15 min, 0.75 mg, and 20 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capability and stability of MNPs@B–N/APBA to capture E. coli O157:H7
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the coexistence of a variety of pathogens in contaminated food and the different conditions of food substrates, we investigated the broad-spectrum recognition performance of MNPs@B–N/APBA, as well as its capture stability to target bacteria under different conditions by using the magnetic separation procedure of MNPs@B–N/APBA to separate bacteria in section 2.1. Firstly, the broad-spectrum recognition ability of MNPs@B–N/APBA on bacteria was investigated. The CE of MNPs@B–N/APBA for most bacteria (104 CFU/mL) was above 90% (Fig. 3 A), whereas the lower CE for C. sakazakii, S. enteritidis and S. typhimurium, which may be attributed to the lower LPS content of their cell wall surfaces [26]. In addition, the CE of MNPs@B–N/APBA for E. coli O157:H7 (104 or 105 CFU/mL) in mixed bacteria was above 90% (Fig. 3B). And MNPs@B–N/APBA had good CE for different concentrations (101–106 CFU/mL) of E. coli O157:H7 in spiked samples (Fig. 3C), and for low concentration of E. coli O157:H7 (103 CFU/mL), the CE could reach more than 84%, indicating that MNPs@B–N/APBA has the ability to capture E. coli O157:H7 in real samples. Finally, the capture stability of MNPs@B–N/APBA for E. coli O157:H7 was investigated. Specifically, we verified the capture ability of MNPs@B–N/APBA for E. coli O157:H7 at different pH (Fig. 3D), salt concentration (Fig. 3E) and storage time (Fig. 3F), and the CEs were all about 90%, indicating that MNPs@B–N/APBA had good capture stability for E. coli O157:H7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of fluorescence signal amplification method for detecting E. coli O157:H7
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the coexistence of multiple pathogens in contaminated food, the specificity of the established biosensor is particularly important. To demonstrate the specificity of the established fluorescence signal amplification assay for E. coli O157:H7, 6 non-target bacteria were detected using the same detection procedure described in section 2.2. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence signals between the control and non-targeted bacteria were essentially consistent, whereas target bacteria or mixture (including target bacteria and others) exhibited intense fluorescence signals, showing that the mAb modified on the surface of the detection probes ensured the specificity of the detection strategy designed for E. coli O157:H7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation the performance of fluorescence signal amplification assay for detecting E. coli O157:H7 in spiked food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, we used a traditional culture method to verify the accuracy of the established method. As shown in Fig. S9, the LOD of the traditional culture method was also 25 CFU/mL in pure culture, spiked lettuce and juice samples. The results show that the established method has a good consistency with the traditional culture method for the detection of E. coli O157:H7, but it has the advantages of shorter detection time and less manpower and material resources.

In conclusion, we established a novel fluorescence signal amplification biosensor for ultrasensitive detection of E. coli O157:H7. A more stable B–N/APBA was synthesized by using the teamed boronate affinity strategy, which was used to synthesize MNPs@B–N/APBA to capture E. coli O157:H7, and then the specific fluorescence signal amplification detection was achieved by labeling and breaking the spore-like mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2 detection probes. A series of characterizations were used to verify the modification of MNPs by B–N/APBA and the synthesis of the mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2. Under the optimized optimal experimental conditions, MNPs@B–N/APBA was able to separate 84.0% of low-abundance E. coli O157:H7 (2.5 × 103 CFU/mL) in lettuce samples. Moreover, combined with specific mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2, we successfully established a fluorescence signal amplification assay for E. coli O157:H7 with the LOD of 25 CFU/mL in pure culture and spiked food samples. The whole detection procedure required approximately 120 min. Based on the excellent bacterial capture ability of the capture probes (MNPs@B–N/APBA) and the good specificity and signal amplification ability of the detection probes (mAb@R-CDs@BONs-NH2), the established fluorescence signal amplification assay showed excellent sensitivity and accuracy. In summary, this study establishes a rapid, stabile and ultrasensitive fluorescent biosensor for the detection of E. coli O157:H7 in food.

Zhiming Ling: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Qian Xu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Yang Song: Formal analysis, Data curation. Wanqing Zhang: Software. Hengyi Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the Research Project of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University (Project No. SKLF-ZZB-202328) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para:
